content:
Before we move forward, it helps to remember where we are in Numbers. Chapter 5 spoke about Yahweh keeping His camp clean and ordered, even in painful matters that humans cannot solve with proof. The law of the loyalty test was written with great detail and seriousness, and its spiritual meaning is best understood when it is explained slowly, not rushed while reading. For those who want a fuller explanation, we encourage you to watch the Journey of Christ series 4 called “The Cup of Judgement”. For now, we will step into the next chapter and simply follow the plain meaning of the text.

Numbers 6 begins with a new section where Yahweh speaks to Moses about a special vow called the Nazirite vow. This vow could be taken by a man or a woman. It was not forced on everyone. It was a voluntary season where someone chose to be set apart to Yahweh in a very visible way. Yahweh gave clear rules, so that the vow would not be vague, and so that the person would not live in confusion.

The first rule is about the grapevine. The Nazirite must give up wine and strong drink. But the command does not stop with wine alone. Yahweh also forbids vinegar made from wine or from strong drink. He forbids fresh grape juice.

He forbids grapes and raisins. He even forbids anything from the grapevine, including seeds and skins. The language is wide and complete, like a fence built all around one area. As long as the vow is active, the Nazirite is not to taste anything that comes from the vine.

The second rule is about the hair. During the days of the vow, no razor is to come upon the head. The Nazirite must let the hair grow long. This is not presented as a private feeling inside the heart. It becomes a public sign.

Anyone who sees the person can recognize that this person is in a separated season to Yahweh. The vow becomes visible, and that visibility brings accountability.

The third rule is about death. The Nazirite must not go near a dead body during the time of separation. Even if the dead person is a close family member; father, mother, brother, or sister; the Nazirite must not defile himself or herself.

The text explains the reason in its own words: the hair on the head is the sign of separation, and the consecration to Yahweh rests upon the head during that season. The vow is treated like holy duty, and death contact would break that holy separation.

Yahweh also provided instructions for a situation that can happen without planning. If someone dies suddenly beside the Nazirite, the consecrated hair becomes defiled. In that case, the Nazirite must wait seven days, and then shave the head. On the eighth day, the Nazirite must bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance of the tabernacle. The priest offers one bird as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. In that way, the Nazirite is purified from the guilt connected with contact with death. Then the person must begin the vow again. The hair starts to grow again, and the days that were completed before the defilement do not count. The vow is restarted for the full time that was originally promised, and the person also brings a one-year-old male lamb for a guilt offering. Yahweh’s law is very clear here:

the separation is serious, and when it is broken by defilement, the vow must be renewed, not treated lightly.

At the conclusion of the Nazirite vow, Yahweh also gives a detailed closing ceremony. The Nazirite comes to the entrance of the tabernacle and brings offerings: a male lamb for a burnt offering, a female lamb for a sin offering, and a ram for a peace offering, along with a basket of unleavened bread, cakes mixed with oil, wafers spread with oil, and the prescribed grain and drink offerings. The priest presents these before Yahweh in an ordered way: first the sin offering and burnt offering, then the peace offering with the bread, and the related offerings as commanded. Then the Nazirite shaves the head at the entrance of the tabernacle and places the dedicated hair on the fire under the peace offering. After that, the priest takes portions that are holy for the priest and places them in the Nazirite’s hands, and lifts them as a special offering before Yahweh. The text then says that after this ceremony the Nazirite may again drink wine. This shows that wine itself is not treated as unclean. It is something given up for a season, because the vow is a season of separation.

The chapter is profound, and Yahweh’s commands are very exact. But one question rises quickly when we read it carefully. Why did Yahweh forbid even vinegar and grapes and raisins? Why did Yahweh close every doorway of the grapevine instead of only saying, “Do not drink wine”? We will study this in tomorrow morning light.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, Holy One of Israel, thank You for Your clear words and Your ordered ways.

May our hearts learn to honor what You call holy and to fear what You call defiling.

May we not treat devotion lightly, and may we not make vows with careless lips.

Teach us to be faithful in small choices and careful in hidden places.

Give us clean thoughts, clean words, and clean steps in the path of obedience.

May Your presence stay near, and may our lives remain set apart to You.

In Christ name, amen.
